subject: UX & Research
title: The Hawaii Missile False Alarm
description: A short lesson to help you apply The Hawaii Missile False Alarm.

hook.text: On January 13, 2018, Hawaiians received an alert: 'BALLISTIC MISSILE THREAT. THIS IS NOT A DRILL.'

buildup.text: An operator clicked the wrong option in a dropdown menu. 'Test missile alert' sat right next to the real one.

discovery.text: The interface had no confirmation dialog. One click sent the alert to every phone in Hawaii.

twist.text: It took 38 minutes to send a correction. People hid in storm drains. Parents pushed children into manholes.

climax.text: Two simple UX fixes could have prevented this: separate test from real options, and add a confirmation step.

punchline.text: Critical actions need confirmation. One wrong click shouldn't cause panic.

quiz.question: What UX failure caused the Hawaii missile false alarm?
options: Test and real alert options were adjacent with no confirmation dialog
The system was hacked by a foreign actor
A software bug automatically triggered the alert
